fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.display_name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Core index fund covering the Nifty Midcap 150 — 150 large and mid-cap Indian companies weighted by market cap. Expense ratio 0.20% — the lowest available for a broad index fund. This is the central growth engine of the portfolio. ₹8L lumpsum + ₹24,000/month SIP = 40% of the plan. The mid-cap index is the right vehicle for Captain Haddock's aggressive risk appetite and 20-year horizon.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Passive Nifty 50 index fund (equivalent of Nifty 50 in Indian markets) backed by the S&P 500 index. This gives Captain Haddock direct exposure to India's large-cap stocks plus global diversification via the US market, all at a 0.20% expense ratio. ₹8L lumpsum + ₹24,000/month SIP = 40% of the plan. At 0.20% ER, this is the lowest-cost route to the Indian large-cap universe.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large Cap Fund (erstwhile Bluechip Fund) - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Satellite allocation — 20% lumpsum only. This fund tracks India's top 100 companies and has ₹13,124 Cr AUM, making it the benchmark large-cap fund in India. Used here only as a lumpsum vehicle since SIPs are already fully covered by the two index funds above. 1.00% ER is higher than index funds but acceptable for a lumpsum only position.

plan.setup_phase: **Month 1:** Deploy ₹8L lumpsum into each of the two index funds (Midcap 150 and Nifty 50) — total ₹16L deployed immediately. Set up SIPs of ₹24,000/month on the 5th of every month into both index funds. Keep ₹4L in ICICI Prudential Large Cap as a lumpsum (not SIP) as the satellite holding. Do not wait for a 'good time' to buy — deploy lumpsums the moment you land in India and set SIPs to run automatically.
review_checkpoints: Year 1 (2026): Confirm all SIPs are running, check if lumpsum allocation was made, review if ₹2L of the ₹2L existing corpus is kept as liquid buffer.
Year 5 (2031): First major review — assess if corpus is on track to reach ₹5Cr by this point, rebalance if equity drifts above 90% (sell into the large-cap satellite fund to trim).
Year 10 (2036): Mid-point check — if the house-buying goal is becoming concrete, start earmarking 30% of growth from the Midcap 150 index for the land purchase. Maintain 70% in equities.
Year 15 (2041): Daughter's education goal — reserve ₹1–1.5Cr in a FD/short-duration debt fund from equity gains. Keep rest in equity for long-term.
Year 20 (2046): Retirement — full reallocation to 60% debt, 30% equity, 10% gold. Begin systematic withdrawal from debt portion.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Review your portfolio **once a year**, preferably in April after the financial year ends. If equity has grown to more than 90% of your total portfolio, sell a small portion into the Nifty 50 index fund (or your existing large-cap fund) to bring equity back to 80%. Do not rebalance more than once a year. Do not rebalance because of market crashes, election results, or RBI policy statements — markets always recover. Rebalancing should be a mechanical, schedule-based activity, not a response to news.
plan.rationale: Captain Haddock, you're 40, earning well, with a 20-year runway and a ₹20L starting corpus. The goal is clear: ₹8Cr by retirement — that's an 11–12% annual return target over 20 years, achievable with a disciplined, low-cost index portfolio. Here's the plan: 100% equity index funds for the next 15 years, with a glide path to 60% equity/35% debt/5% gold by retirement. 80% of your money is in two passive index funds (Nifty 50 and Nifty Midcap 150) at 0.20% ER each — the lowest possible cost. 20% of your lumpsum is in a large-cap index fund as a stable core. Total portfolio expense ratio: ~0.20%, far below the 1%+ that active funds charge. With a ₹20L existing corpus, lumpsum deployment of ₹16L (80% each) plus monthly SIPs of ₹60,000 (split 24K each) sets you on track for ₹8Cr by retirement at 11% CAGR. The step-up SIP plan (10% annual increase) is your prosperity accelerator — it works silently while you're on duty, growing your investment faster as your income grows.
risks: Markets can fall 30–40% in a bad year (2008, 2020). If you're at sea during such a crash, you'll miss the panic — that's a feature, not a bug. Don't stop SIPs or sell in a panic.
Inflation risk: ₹8Cr today may not buy the same house or education in 20 years. Keep equity allocation high and live modestly while you're earning and compounding.
Currency risk for your daughter's education abroad: USD-denominated education costs can rise fast. Gold and debt provide a hedge.
Lumpsum timing risk: The ₹20L corpus is already invested. Deploying the remaining lumpsum at any time is fine — markets are efficient and you cannot time them reliably.
Regulatory changes: Tax rules on LTCG (Long-Term Capital Gains) can change. Review your tax plan every 3 years.
House purchase risk: Land and construction costs in India are volatile. Set aside 30–40% of equity gains for this goal early — don't wait until you're 55 to buy land.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase each SIP by 10% every year — from ₹24,000/month to ₹26,400/month in Year 2, ₹29,040/month in Year 3, and so on. This step-up roughly matches salary growth (assuming your income increases by ~10% annually) and significantly accelerates the corpus build-up. Even a 10% step-up on ₹24,000/month SIP will double your monthly investment by the 10th year, compounding into a massive retirement corpus.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Starting allocation — aggressive 100% equity index fund portfolio
10-year mark — begin gradual de-risking, move 15% into debt from equity gains
15-year mark — 5 years to retirement, shift 40% of equity gains to debt and gold
Retirement — final reallocation before drawing down corpus

plan.perspective: Captain Haddock, you're in a unique position: you earn well but are away from India 8 months a year. That works to your advantage. Your lump sum investments — deployed when you're ashore — can be the biggest accelerator of your wealth. For the other 4 months at sea, your SIPs keep running automatically, compounding your smaller monthly savings. The good news is that at 40 with a 20-year horizon and an aggressive risk profile, you can — and should — be almost fully in equity index funds. Your goals — ₹8Cr corpus, buying land, funding your daughter's education — all take 20 years. You have time to ride out the ups and downs of markets. The biggest risk to your plan isn't a bad year for stocks — it's doing nothing at all.

aps.reasoning: This is an overwhelmingly passive investment plan. 80% of equity allocation is in pure index funds (Nifty Midcap 150 and S&P 500 Index Fund) with 0.2% expense ratios. The remaining 20% is in a large-cap fund but used primarily for rebalancing purposes. The plan emphasizes annual rebalancing only, minimal cost focus with 0.2% expense ratios, no individual stock research (broad market exposure only), 20-year long-term horizon, and portfolio metrics showing index-like behavior (beta close to 1.0 for midcap fund, low beta for international fund). The rationale explicitly champions passive investing with low costs and mechanical rebalancing.

pqs.reasoning: Goal alignment: Excellent (0.9) - Plan aligns well with aggressive risk appetite, 20-year horizon, and 8Cr retirement goal. Uses index funds as preferred, includes lump sum approach matching investor's availability pattern. Diversification: Good but flawed (0.7) - Broad equity exposure across midcap, large cap, and international (S&P 500). However, there's a significant inconsistency: rationale mentions "Nifty 50" but fund is actually S&P 500 index fund (US market), creating geographic concentration risk. Risk-return: Very good (0.8) - Appropriate for aggressive investor with 20-year horizon. 100% equity allocation matches risk appetite. Projected 11.5% CAGR seems reasonable for long-term equity. Internal consistency: Problematic (0.6) - Major contradiction: rationale describes Motilal Oswal S&P 500 fund as "passive Nifty 50 index fund" when it's actually tracking US S&P 500. This creates confusion about geographic exposure. Tax efficiency: Excellent (0.9) - Investor is on new_regime tax bracket, so plan correctly avoids ELSS recommendation. All funds are equity-focused with favorable tax treatment (LTCG 12.5% above ₹1.25L), appropriate for new_regime investor.
